videos.title: The major scale: eight steps, seven names
Intervals from the tonic: number and quality

exercises.title: Ear-tests in C major
exercises.instructions: Each phrase stays inside one octave of the major scale. The tonic is given and sounded first; hear the phrase played, sing it back on degree names, then write it down. Do not write what you cannot hear.

exercises.title: Ear-tests in C major
exercises.instructions: Each phrase stays inside one octave of the major scale. The tonic is given and sounded first; hear the phrase played, sing it back on degree names, then write it down. Do not write what you cannot hear.

exercises.title: Intervals between the degrees of C major
exercises.instructions: Each pair names two degrees of the scale; the first note is given. Write the second note, reckoning the interval upwards, and name its number and quality (e.g. minor third) before you play it. All intervals lie within one octave.

exercises.title: Intervals between the degrees of C major
exercises.instructions: Each pair names two degrees of the scale; the first note is given. Write the second note, reckoning the interval upwards, and name its number and quality (e.g. minor third) before you play it. All intervals lie within one octave.

videos.title: Four voices, three notes: arranging a triad

exercises.title: Write the cadences in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each named cadence for four voices, root position, the final chord on the strong accent: perfect V–I, plagal IV–I, imperfect (half-close) ending on V. All the connection rules hold; the leading note rises at the perfect cadence.

exercises.title: Write the cadences in G major
exercises.instructions: Write each named cadence for four voices, root position, the final chord on the strong accent: perfect V–I, plagal IV–I, imperfect (half-close) ending on V. All the connection rules hold; the leading note rises at the perfect cadence.

exercises.title: Arrange the primary triads in C major
exercises.instructions: The bass note (the root) is given twice for each chord. Add soprano, alto, and tenor, making two different arrangements: complete chord, root or fifth doubled — not the major third, never the leading note. Keep adjacent upper parts within an octave. (The book labels root position Ia, IVa, Va; we write I, IV, V.)

exercises.title: Arrange the primary triads in C major
exercises.instructions: The bass note (the root) is given twice for each chord. Add soprano, alto, and tenor, making two different arrangements: complete chord, root or fifth doubled — not the major third, never the leading note. Keep adjacent upper parts within an octave. (The book labels root position Ia, IVa, Va; we write I, IV, V.)

exercises.title: Add the inner parts in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor, forming primary triads in root position. Keep the common tone in the same part, move to the nearest note, no consecutive fifths or octaves, and let the leading note rise.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor, forming primary triads in root position. Keep the common tone in the same part, move to the nearest note, no consecutive fifths or octaves, and let the leading note rise.

exercises.title: Write the cadences in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each named cadence for four voices, root position, the final chord on the strong accent: perfect V–I, plagal IV–I, imperfect (half-close) ending on V. All the connection rules hold; the leading note rises at the perfect cadence.

exercises.title: Write the cadences in E minor
exercises.instructions: Write each named cadence for four voices, root position, the final chord on the strong accent: perfect V–I, plagal IV–I, imperfect (half-close) ending on V. All the connection rules hold; the leading note rises at the perfect cadence.

exercises.title: The Tierce de Picardie in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each close for four voices, sharpening the third of the final tonic chord (Tierce de Picardie) — the phrase ends major in a minor key. Watch the leading note (it still rises) and never move by an augmented second.

exercises.title: The Tierce de Picardie in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each close for four voices, sharpening the third of the final tonic chord (Tierce de Picardie) — the phrase ends major in a minor key. Watch the leading note (it still rises) and never move by an augmented second.

exercises.title: Arrange the primary triads in A minor
exercises.instructions: The bass note (the root) is given twice for each chord. Add soprano, alto, and tenor, making two different arrangements: complete chord, root or fifth doubled — not the major third, never the leading note. Keep adjacent upper parts within an octave. (The book labels root position Ia, IVa, Va; we write I, IV, V.)

exercises.title: Arrange the primary triads in A minor
exercises.instructions: The bass note (the root) is given twice for each chord. Add soprano, alto, and tenor, making two different arrangements: complete chord, root or fifth doubled — not the major third, never the leading note. Keep adjacent upper parts within an octave. (The book labels root position Ia, IVa, Va; we write I, IV, V.)

exercises.title: Add the inner parts in A minor
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor, forming primary triads in root position. Keep the common tone in the same part, move to the nearest note, no consecutive fifths or octaves, and let the leading note rise.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts in A minor
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor, forming primary triads in root position. Keep the common tone in the same part, move to the nearest note, no consecutive fifths or octaves, and let the leading note rise.

videos.title: Two new chords: the supertonic and submediant triads

exercises.title: Approaching the closes in C minor
exercises.instructions: Write each three-chord formula for four voices. At the false close (V to VI) the leading note rises and the third of VI is doubled; avoid IIa to Ia everywhere; upper parts against the bass wherever the roots move by step.

exercises.title: Approaching the closes in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each three-chord formula for four voices. At the false close (V to VI) the leading note rises and the third of VI is doubled; avoid IIa to Ia everywhere; upper parts against the bass wherever the roots move by step.

exercises.title: Approaching the closes in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each three-chord formula for four voices. At the false close (V to VI) the leading note rises and the third of VI is doubled; avoid IIa to Ia everywhere; upper parts against the bass wherever the roots move by step.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (with II and VI) in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. The vocabulary now includes the supertonic and submediant triads: same habits as before, plus — never IIa to Ia, and at V to VI double the third of VI.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (with II and VI) in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. The vocabulary now includes the supertonic and submediant triads: same habits as before, plus — never IIa to Ia, and at V to VI double the third of VI.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (with II and VI) in G major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. The vocabulary now includes the supertonic and submediant triads: same habits as before, plus — never IIa to Ia, and at V to VI double the third of VI.

videos.title: The first inversion: the third in the bass
What the sixth chord is for

exercises.title: Cadences through first inversions in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each formula for four voices; the chord marked 6 stands in first inversion, its bass carrying the third. The stepwise bass into the cadence is the point — smoother than root positions alone.

exercises.title: Cadences through first inversions in G major
exercises.instructions: Write each formula for four voices; the chord marked 6 stands in first inversion, its bass carrying the third. The stepwise bass into the cadence is the point — smoother than root positions alone.

exercises.title: Root positions and first inversions in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. A label with 6 marks a first inversion — the bass carries the chord's third. Doubling follows the root-position principle; never VIIb to Va; watch the fifths where VIIb is involved.

exercises.title: Root positions and first inversions in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. A label with 6 marks a first inversion — the bass carries the chord's third. Doubling follows the root-position principle; never VIIb to Va; watch the fifths where VIIb is involved.

exercises.title: Arrange the first inversions in C major
exercises.instructions: Each given bass note is the THIRD of its chord — the figure 6 beneath a bass means a first inversion (6/3). Add soprano, alto, and tenor two different ways: complete the chord and double the root or the fifth (never the leading note).

exercises.title: Arrange the first inversions in C major
exercises.instructions: Each given bass note is the THIRD of its chord — the figure 6 beneath a bass means a first inversion (6/3). Add soprano, alto, and tenor two different ways: complete the chord and double the root or the fifth (never the leading note).

videos.title: The first inversions of the minor key
Minor cadences through the sixth chords

exercises.title: Cadences through first inversions in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each formula for four voices; the chord marked 6 stands in first inversion, its bass carrying the third. The stepwise bass into the cadence is the point — smoother than root positions alone.

exercises.title: Cadences through first inversions in D minor
exercises.instructions: Write each formula for four voices; the chord marked 6 stands in first inversion, its bass carrying the third. The stepwise bass into the cadence is the point — smoother than root positions alone.

exercises.title: Root positions and first inversions in A minor
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. A label with 6 marks a first inversion — the bass carries the chord's third. Doubling follows the root-position principle; never VIIb to Va; watch the fifths where VIIb is involved.

exercises.title: Root positions and first inversions in A minor
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. A label with 6 marks a first inversion — the bass carries the chord's third. Doubling follows the root-position principle; never VIIb to Va; watch the fifths where VIIb is involved.

exercises.title: Arrange the first inversions in A minor
exercises.instructions: Each given bass note is the THIRD of its chord — the figure 6 beneath a bass means a first inversion (6/3). Add soprano, alto, and tenor two different ways: complete the chord and double the root or the fifth (never the leading note).

exercises.title: Arrange the first inversions in A minor
exercises.instructions: Each given bass note is the THIRD of its chord — the figure 6 beneath a bass means a first inversion (6/3). Add soprano, alto, and tenor two different ways: complete the chord and double the root or the fifth (never the leading note).

exercises.title: Harmonize the melodic minor in A minor
exercises.instructions: The soprano is given; harmonize it in four voices. Going up, the raised sixth avoids the augmented second — harmonize it as II (with its perfect fifth) or IV (with its raised third), and never double it. Coming down, the flattened seventh serves III, v, or VII; the true leading note returns a chord later. The two forms of a degree never meet in consecutive chords.

exercises.title: Harmonize the melodic minor in A minor
exercises.instructions: The soprano is given; harmonize it in four voices. Going up, the raised sixth avoids the augmented second — harmonize it as II (with its perfect fifth) or IV (with its raised third), and never double it. Coming down, the flattened seventh serves III, v, or VII; the true leading note returns a chord later. The two forms of a degree never meet in consecutive chords.

exercises.title: Harmonize the melodic minor in D minor
exercises.instructions: The soprano is given; harmonize it in four voices. Going up, the raised sixth avoids the augmented second — harmonize it as II (with its perfect fifth) or IV (with its raised third), and never double it. Coming down, the flattened seventh serves III, v, or VII; the true leading note returns a chord later. The two forms of a degree never meet in consecutive chords.

exercises.title: Harmonize the melodic minor in E minor
exercises.instructions: The soprano is given; harmonize it in four voices. Going up, the raised sixth avoids the augmented second — harmonize it as II (with its perfect fifth) or IV (with its raised third), and never double it. Coming down, the flattened seventh serves III, v, or VII; the true leading note returns a chord later. The two forms of a degree never meet in consecutive chords.

videos.title: The passing trap: fifths out of nowhere

exercises.title: Add the inner parts under a moving soprano in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; the unaccented soprano note approached and quitted by step is a passing note — no chord of its own. Add alto and tenor as whole notes beneath the moving line, all the usual rules holding.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts under a moving soprano in C major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; the unaccented soprano note approached and quitted by step is a passing note — no chord of its own. Add alto and tenor as whole notes beneath the moving line, all the usual rules holding.

exercises.title: Add the passing notes in C major
exercises.instructions: Each pair of chords is given complete; the soprano moves by a third. Re-write the passage, filling the third with an unaccented diatonic passing note on the weak half of the measure. A passing note is approached and quitted by step in one direction, never makes an augmented second, and must not produce consecutive fifths.

exercises.title: Add the passing notes in C major
exercises.instructions: Each pair of chords is given complete; the soprano moves by a third. Re-write the passage, filling the third with an unaccented diatonic passing note on the weak half of the measure. A passing note is approached and quitted by step in one direction, never makes an augmented second, and must not produce consecutive fifths.

exercises.title: Add the passing notes in G major
exercises.instructions: Each pair of chords is given complete; the soprano moves by a third. Re-write the passage, filling the third with an unaccented diatonic passing note on the weak half of the measure. A passing note is approached and quitted by step in one direction, never makes an augmented second, and must not produce consecutive fifths.

videos.title: Curious passing notes: the minor key's workarounds
Two passing notes in a row

exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; the unaccented soprano note approached and quitted by step is a passing note — no chord of its own. Add alto and tenor as whole notes beneath the moving line, all the usual rules holding.

Re-write the passage, filling the third with an unaccented diatonic passing note on the weak half of the measure. A passing note is approached and quitted by step in one direction, never makes an augmented second, and must not produce consecutive fifths.

exercises.title: Add the passing notes in E minor
exercises.instructions: Each pair of chords is given complete; the soprano moves by a third. Re-write the passage, filling the third with an unaccented diatonic passing note on the weak half of the measure. A passing note is approached and quitted by step in one direction, never makes an augmented second, and must not produce consecutive fifths.

videos.title: Passing, auxiliary, arpeggio: the other six-fours

exercises.title: Closes decorated by the six-four in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each close with its cadential six-four. The six-four stands on the tonic or dominant bass only, more strongly accented than the chord that resolves it: the sixth falls to the fifth, the fourth to the third, and the bass note is doubled in both chords.

exercises.title: Closes decorated by the six-four in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each close with its cadential six-four. The six-four stands on the tonic or dominant bass only, more strongly accented than the chord that resolves it: the sixth falls to the fifth, the fourth to the third, and the bass note is doubled in both chords.

exercises.title: Closes decorated by the six-four in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each close with its cadential six-four. The six-four stands on the tonic or dominant bass only, more strongly accented than the chord that resolves it: the sixth falls to the fifth, the fourth to the third, and the bass note is doubled in both chords.

exercises.title: The six-four's other uses in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each cell. Passing: the six-four stands between two positions of one chord, the soprano taking the bass's notes in reverse order. Auxiliary: the bass holds while the sixth and fourth wave above it. Arpeggio: the bass walks its own chord. Use the six-four sparingly — it must always account for itself.

exercises.title: The six-four's other uses in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each cell. Passing: the six-four stands between two positions of one chord, the soprano taking the bass's notes in reverse order. Auxiliary: the bass holds while the sixth and fourth wave above it. Arpeggio: the bass walks its own chord. Use the six-four sparingly — it must always account for itself.

exercises.title: The six-four's other uses in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each cell. Passing: the six-four stands between two positions of one chord, the soprano taking the bass's notes in reverse order. Auxiliary: the bass holds while the sixth and fourth wave above it. Arpeggio: the bass walks its own chord. Use the six-four sparingly — it must always account for itself.

videos.title: The passing note moves onto the beat
The appendix case: G sharp without modulation

exercises.title: Accented passing notes in A minor
exercises.instructions: The soprano falls a third between the chords. Re-write each pair, striking the intermediate note WITH the second chord — on the strong half — and resolving it down by step. Never strike the discord against its own resolution note unless that note lies in the bass.

exercises.title: Accented passing notes in C major
exercises.instructions: The soprano falls a third between the chords. Re-write each pair, striking the intermediate note WITH the second chord — on the strong half — and resolving it down by step. Never strike the discord against its own resolution note unless that note lies in the bass.

exercises.title: Accented passing notes in C major
exercises.instructions: The soprano falls a third between the chords. Re-write each pair, striking the intermediate note WITH the second chord — on the strong half — and resolving it down by step. Never strike the discord against its own resolution note unless that note lies in the bass.

exercises.title: Accented passing notes in G major
exercises.instructions: The soprano falls a third between the chords. Re-write each pair, striking the intermediate note WITH the second chord — on the strong half — and resolving it down by step. Never strike the discord against its own resolution note unless that note lies in the bass.

exercises.title: The dominant seventh in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each cell: the dominant seventh in the position named, properly quitted. The seventh falls one step (from the 4/3 it may rise in parallels with the bass); the leading note rises; the 2 sends its bass down to a chord of the sixth. In root position the fifth may be omitted and the root doubled.

exercises.title: The dominant seventh in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell: the dominant seventh in the position named, properly quitted. The seventh falls one step (from the 4/3 it may rise in parallels with the bass); the leading note rises; the 2 sends its bass down to a chord of the sixth. In root position the fifth may be omitted and the root doubled.

exercises.title: The dominant seventh in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell: the dominant seventh in the position named, properly quitted. The seventh falls one step (from the 4/3 it may rise in parallels with the bass); the leading note rises; the 2 sends its bass down to a chord of the sixth. In root position the fifth may be omitted and the root doubled.

exercises.title: The dominant seventh in G major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell: the dominant seventh in the position named, properly quitted. The seventh falls one step (from the 4/3 it may rise in parallels with the bass); the leading note rises; the 2 sends its bass down to a chord of the sixth. In root position the fifth may be omitted and the root doubled.

videos.title: The diminished triad in root position

exercises.title: The discord triads in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. The diminished fifth over the bass must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation), must fall one step into the chord after (resolution), and is never doubled. The augmented fifth — the leading note itself — is prepared the same way and rises.

exercises.title: The discord triads in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. The diminished fifth over the bass must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation), must fall one step into the chord after (resolution), and is never doubled. The augmented fifth — the leading note itself — is prepared the same way and rises.

exercises.title: The discord triads in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. The diminished fifth over the bass must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation), must fall one step into the chord after (resolution), and is never doubled. The augmented fifth — the leading note itself — is prepared the same way and rises.

exercises.title: The discord triads in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. The diminished fifth over the bass must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation), must fall one step into the chord after (resolution), and is never doubled. The augmented fifth — the leading note itself — is prepared the same way and rises.

exercises.title: The discord triads in E minor
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. The diminished fifth over the bass must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation), must fall one step into the chord after (resolution), and is never doubled. The augmented fifth — the leading note itself — is prepared the same way and rises.

exercises.title: The mediant triad in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. In root position the mediant keeps a common tone with both neighbours — except falling to IV under the descending scale. As iii6 it may stand wherever the dominant could, provided the sixth above the bass falls a step or a third.

exercises.title: The mediant triad in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. In root position the mediant keeps a common tone with both neighbours — except falling to IV under the descending scale. As iii6 it may stand wherever the dominant could, provided the sixth above the bass falls a step or a third.

exercises.title: The mediant triad in F major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. In root position the mediant keeps a common tone with both neighbours — except falling to IV under the descending scale. As iii6 it may stand wherever the dominant could, provided the sixth above the bass falls a step or a third.

exercises.title: The mediant triad in G major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. In root position the mediant keeps a common tone with both neighbours — except falling to IV under the descending scale. As iii6 it may stand wherever the dominant could, provided the sixth above the bass falls a step or a third.

videos.title: The lower neighbour and its distances

exercises.title: Auxiliary notes in A minor
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, letting the soprano leave its tone by step and return to it. Upper auxiliaries are diatonic; lower ones lie a semitone below a root or fifth, a tone or semitone below a third. The harmony holds beneath the ornament.

exercises.title: Auxiliary notes in A minor
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, letting the soprano leave its tone by step and return to it. Upper auxiliaries are diatonic; lower ones lie a semitone below a root or fifth, a tone or semitone below a third. The harmony holds beneath the ornament.

exercises.title: Auxiliary notes in C major
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, letting the soprano leave its tone by step and return to it. Upper auxiliaries are diatonic; lower ones lie a semitone below a root or fifth, a tone or semitone below a third. The harmony holds beneath the ornament.

exercises.title: Auxiliary notes in C major
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, letting the soprano leave its tone by step and return to it. Upper auxiliaries are diatonic; lower ones lie a semitone below a root or fifth, a tone or semitone below a third. The harmony holds beneath the ornament.

exercises.title: Auxiliary notes in G major
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, letting the soprano leave its tone by step and return to it. Upper auxiliaries are diatonic; lower ones lie a semitone below a root or fifth, a tone or semitone below a third. The harmony holds beneath the ornament.

exercises.title: Cadences approached through II7 in Bb major
exercises.instructions: Write each formula for four voices. The supertonic seventh is prepared by the chord before it; its seventh falls to the leading note at the dominant chord — or holds through the cadential six-four as the fourth from the bass, falling after. All the Part I cadence habits stand.

exercises.title: Cadences approached through II7 in E minor
exercises.instructions: Write each formula for four voices. The supertonic seventh is prepared by the chord before it; its seventh falls to the leading note at the dominant chord — or holds through the cadential six-four as the fourth from the bass, falling after. All the Part I cadence habits stand.

exercises.title: Continue the chain of sevenths in F major
exercises.instructions: The first two chords give the pattern; continue the sequence to the end. The roots fall in fifths, the third of each chord remains in its part to become the seventh of the next, and every seventh falls one step onto the third of the chord after. Complete and incomplete chords alternate; a leading note doubled by the repetition of the pattern is allowable.

exercises.title: Continue the chain of sevenths in G major
exercises.instructions: The first two chords give the pattern; continue the sequence to the end. The roots fall in fifths, the third of each chord remains in its part to become the seventh of the next, and every seventh falls one step onto the third of the chord after. Complete and incomplete chords alternate; a leading note doubled by the repetition of the pattern is allowable.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (with diatonic sevenths) in D minor
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. Every chord marked with a 7 is a diatonic seventh: prepare the seventh in the same part, and let it fall one step onto the third of the next chord (or hold it through the cadential six-four). The Part I connection rules all hold.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (with diatonic sevenths) in G major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. Every chord marked with a 7 is a diatonic seventh: prepare the seventh in the same part, and let it fall one step onto the third of the next chord (or hold it through the cadential six-four). The Part I connection rules all hold.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (with diatonic sevenths) in G major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. Every chord marked with a 7 is a diatonic seventh: prepare the seventh in the same part, and let it fall one step onto the third of the next chord (or hold it through the cadential six-four). The Part I connection rules all hold.

exercises.title: Prepare and resolve the diatonic sevenths in A minor
exercises.instructions: Write each cell for four voices. The seventh must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation) and must fall one step into a chord of which that note is the third — the roots fall a fifth. Before a cadential six-four the seventh may hold over as the fourth from the bass and fall afterwards.

exercises.title: Prepare and resolve the diatonic sevenths in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell for four voices. The seventh must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation) and must fall one step into a chord of which that note is the third — the roots fall a fifth. Before a cadential six-four the seventh may hold over as the fourth from the bass and fall afterwards.

exercises.title: Prepare and resolve the diatonic sevenths in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell for four voices. The seventh must already stand in the same part in the chord before (preparation) and must fall one step into a chord of which that note is the third — the roots fall a fifth. Before a cadential six-four the seventh may hold over as the fourth from the bass and fall afterwards.

videos.title: The chord of the added sixth

exercises.title: The chord of the added sixth in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each close for four voices. The supertonic seventh in first inversion is heard as the subdominant chord with a sixth added above the bass; it resolves directly to the tonic, and the seventh — for once — does not fall. It may enter unprepared.

exercises.title: The chord of the added sixth in G minor
exercises.instructions: Write each close for four voices. The supertonic seventh in first inversion is heard as the subdominant chord with a sixth added above the bass; it resolves directly to the tonic, and the seventh — for once — does not fall. It may enter unprepared.

exercises.title: The borrowed supertonic seventh in A major
exercises.instructions: Write each cadence using II7 of the minor key inside the major: the supertonic seventh with the flattened sixth degree (a chromatic tone on loan). Prepare its seventh as usual and let it fall to the leading note — or hold it through the cadential six-four. Keep the borrowed tone out of the neighbouring chords.

exercises.title: Freed resolutions of the sevenths in C major
exercises.instructions: Write each cell. The seventh still falls one step, but the receiving chord is free: IV7 goes to V (or holds through the six-four), VI7 to the dominant seventh, VII7 to V7 or straight to I. These sevenths may enter unprepared; the bass quits each by step or stays where it is. Never two parts from 7 to 8.

exercises.title: Add the inner parts (particular uses) in D major
exercises.instructions: Soprano and bass are given; add alto and tenor. The phrases lean on the particular uses: the added sixth, IV7 into V, VI7 into the dominant seventh, the leading and diminished sevenths. These sevenths may enter unprepared — but each still falls (the added sixth alone stands still), and the bass quits each by step.

exercises.title: Precede and follow the sevenths in A minor
exercises.instructions: Each middle chord is given complete. Write a chord before it and a chord after it in the same key: the approach prepares the seventh (or lawfully takes it free, for the particular uses of ch. II), and the resolution receives the falling discord. The answer shows one good way; others exist.

exercises.title: Anticipations in A major
exercises.instructions: The soprano moves by step between the chords. Re-write each pair, sounding the coming tone early on the last quarter of the first measure — the anticipation. It belongs in the top part, and no other harmony note is struck with it.

exercises.title: Anticipations in D minor
exercises.instructions: The soprano moves by step between the chords. Re-write each pair, sounding the coming tone early on the last quarter of the first measure — the anticipation. It belongs in the top part, and no other harmony note is struck with it.

exercises.title: Changing notes in A minor
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, decorating the soprano as named. The neighbour group takes the steps above and below the harmony note in either order (or curtailed, without the initial note); the third-figures bridge two chord tones with the step below (or above) each. Below a root or fifth the lower neighbour lies a semitone; the harmony holds beneath.

exercises.title: Changing notes in C major
exercises.instructions: Re-write each chord, decorating the soprano as named. The neighbour group takes the steps above and below the harmony note in either order (or curtailed, without the initial note); the third-figures bridge two chord tones with the step below (or above) each. exercises.title: Chromatic passing notes in Bb major
exercises.instructions: The soprano rises a whole tone between the chords. Re-write each pair, filling the step with a chromatic passing note on the weak half: once introduced, the part proceeds in semitones until it reaches a harmony note. Keep clear of the semitone below a chord's major third — it shadows the minor third.

exercises.title: Chromatic passing notes in G major
exercises.instructions: The soprano rises a whole tone between the chords. Re-write each pair, filling the step with a chromatic passing note on the weak half: once introduced, the part proceeds in semitones until it reaches a harmony note. Keep clear of the semitone below a chord's major third — it shadows the minor third.

exercises.title: Modulate from B minor to its related keys
exercises.instructions: Write each modulation in four chords: the home tonic; a pivot chord approached in the old key and quitted in the new (its double name is given); then the new key's dominant and tonic. The confirmation must carry a note foreign to the key quitted — where the plain dominant has none, its minor seventh is added. Chromatic alterations stay in one part (no false relation).

exercises.title: Transitions from D major
exercises.instructions: Write each transition in three chords: the old tonic set directly against the new key's dominant seventh — no common chord between them, only a common note kept in its part — then the new tonic. Chromatic alterations stay in one voice. Transitions serve mid-phrase; never at the end of a normal phrase.

exercises.title: Plan a sixteen-bar period in C major
exercises.instructions: Compose a 16-bar period (two 8-bar sentences) to the scheme given: establish the tonic through the first phrase; end the second with a full close in the key named (never the flat side); let the third phrase visit the flat-side keys, ideally as two sequential sub-sections; and bring the fourth phrase home gradually. The answer shows each phrase's closing cadence as a signpost — the bars between are yours.

exercises.title: Bass suspensions in D minor
exercises.instructions: The bass falls one step between the chords. Re-write the second measure, holding the old bass under the new upper parts — they must figure 5/2 (or 4/2) above it, and none of them may sound the bass's note of resolution — then let the bass make its step.

exercises.title: Bass suspensions in G major
exercises.instructions: The bass falls one step between the chords. Re-write the second measure, holding the old bass under the new upper parts — they must figure 5/2 (or 4/2) above it, and none of them may sound the bass's note of resolution — then let the bass make its step.

exercises.title: Ornamental resolutions in E minor
exercises.instructions: Each second measure carries a 9-8 suspension. Resolve it ornamentally as named: leap or step to another factor of the chord before falling; touch the lower auxiliary first; or sound the resolution, dip below it, and settle. The harmony holds throughout the ornament.

exercises.title: Ornamental resolutions in F major
exercises.instructions: Each second measure carries a 9-8 suspension. Resolve it ornamentally as named: leap or step to another factor of the chord before falling; touch the lower auxiliary first; or sound the resolution, dip below it, and settle. The harmony holds throughout the ornament.

exercises.title: Prepare and resolve the suspensions in A minor
exercises.instructions: Each pair of chords is given plain; the marked voice falls (or, in the 7-8, rises a semitone) between them. Re-write the second measure, retarding that voice: the old tone strikes WITH the new chord on the strong half and resolves on the weak. The discord must be prepared, must be more strongly accented than its resolution, and must not sound against the resolution note unless that note is in the bass.

exercises.title: The enharmonic diminished seventh from F major
exercises.instructions: The same diminished-seventh SOUND serves several keys: it is the dominant minor ninth with its root omitted, and any of its four tones can be re-read as the third, fifth, seventh, or ninth of a new dominant. Resolve the given chord in each key named, writing it in the notation of the key approached.

exercises.title: The dominant ninth in A minor
exercises.instructions: Treat the ninth three ways. Prepared and resolving over the sustained dominant, it is a suspension; unprepared, an appoggiatura — in both, the root may sound against it only in the bass. As the TRUE chord of the ninth it resolves one step down onto new harmony: the fifth stays out in four parts, the major ninth sounds above the third, and no two parts run from a ninth to an octave.

exercises.title: The dominant thirteenth in A minor
exercises.instructions: The outer chords are given; write the dominant thirteenth between them. Four parts: root, third, seventh, thirteenth — the thirteenth stands in for the fifth and must sound above the seventh. Its exits: fall one step to the fifth while the dominant stands; leap down direct to the tonic root (the true chord); or hold as a common tone of the chord that follows. A true dominant discord always carries its seventh.
